input.background: In Example 8.4 (Section 8.2), the two-sample $t$-statistic was used to compare the success indexes of two groups of managers. The data are repeated in Table 8.9. The use of the $t$-statistic was based on the assumption that the population variances of the managerial success indexes were equal for the two groups.
input.data:
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline \multicolumn{12}{|l|}{Table 8.9 Managerial Success Indexes for Two Groups of Managers} \\
\hline \multicolumn{6}{|c|}{Group 1} & \multicolumn{6}{|c|}{Group 2} \\
\hline \multicolumn{6}{|c|}{Interaction with Outsiders} & \multicolumn{6}{|c|}{Few Interactions} \\
\hline 65 & 58 & 78 & 60 & 68 & 69 & 62 & 53 & 36 & 34 & 56 & 50 \\
\hline 66 & 70 & 53 & 71 & 63 & 63 & 42 & 57 & 46 & 68 & 48 & 42 \\
\hline & & & & & & 52 & 53 & 43 & & & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
input.question: Explain the limitations of the $F$-test in this context and why we cannot conclude that the data justify the use of the $t$-statistic.

output.answer: It is here that the temptation to misuse the $F$-test is strongest. We cannot conclude that the data justify the use of the $t$-statistic. This is equivalent to accepting $H_0$, and we have repeatedly warned against this conclusion because the probability of a Type II error, $\beta$, is unknown. The $\alpha$ level of .10 protects us only against rejecting $H_0$ if it is true. This use of the $F$-test may prevent us from abusing the $t$-procedure when we obtain a value of $F$ that leads to a rejection of the assumption that $\sigma_1^2=\sigma_2^2$. But when the $F$-statistic does not fall in the rejection region, we know little more about the validity of the assumption than before we conducted the test.

meta_info.caption: Checking the Assumption of Equal Variances
meta_info.section: 8.6 Comparing Two Population Variances: Independent Sampling
